For shorter extracts, the ones that are chosen need no modernizing, like \u201c\u2019the swyftnesse and the sweighe\u2019 of worldly fortune\u2019s \u2018turnynge wheel\u2019\u201d (p. 105) and \u201c\u2019as stil as any ston\u2019\u201d (p. 107.) Longer extracts are isolated from the narrative to indicate that their modern translations are to be found at the back of the book. Some of these may be easily read, nevertheless, without help from the back: \u201cThe lyf so short, the craft so long to lerne,/Th\u2019assay so hard, so sharp the conqueryng\u201d (p. 88.) Through experimentation and by the influence of Petrarch, Boccaccio, and Dante (pp. 44, 45), Chaucer achieved this \u2018high style,\u2019 which later poets were to imitate for centuries.

The extract from page 88 seems to be about what is elsewhere termed \u2018the craft of fyn lovynge\u2019 (p. 32.) But it is a fit description of the arc and process of Chaucer\u2019s composition. \u201cEpilogues are cancelled\u2026prologues are rewritten\u2026lines from one tale\u2026added to another\u201d (p. 141.) Struggling writers will be relieved to read that. They will be interested to learn, moreover, that Chaucer relied on several tactics to carry out his writing ambitions. He fashioned characters from what he observed in certain people (p. 46.) Sometimes he conveyed credibility by injecting himself into his work (p. 145.) He disarmed criticism by self-deprecation (p. 144.) And he may have feigned more ignorance in his subject than was the case in order to come across more remarkably (p. 86.) Given the fact that a criminal could, in that day, escape the death penalty on the ground of being able to read (pp. 135, 136), a writer, especially one as talented as Chaucer was, must have been a man set apart from the crowd.

Chaucer had the advantage of being born of well-to-do parents, and served the royal court from his youth onward (pp. xiii, 1.) The fringe benefits that he enjoyed from the crown were many: rent-free property, grants, and annuities, for example (pp. 46, 52.) As \u2018controller of the wool custom,\u2019 he had one of the plum jobs of the day (p. 49.) That said, plum job appointments were not as mellow as they now are. Overseeing the wool trade \u2018was not an easy sinecure\u2019 (Ibid.) And that was only one role out of many that he filled. Some of the business that he did for the crown was positively perilous. What can \u2018clandestine diplomatic business\u2019 (p. 25) mean but that he was a spy for the king? Once he was even captured on the battlefield (p. 19.) During a royal disruption some of his peers were beheaded (p. 129.) The Chaucer who emerges from this biography is not the soft, merely bookish man that I supposed him to have been. He was a daring court official who wrote poetry on the side (p. 62.) Fourteenth century England was a tough, dangerous, and fairly lawless place and period (pp. 2, 10, 40, 41, 81, 83, 96, 97, 128, 154.) Chaucer was in the thick of it with everyone else.

Facts dropped in here and there by the author cause us to involuntarily compare Chaucer\u2019s England with where we now are so many years hence. The \u2018Stocks market\u2019 used to be about \u2018fish and flesh\u2019 (p. 5.) A penthouse used to be a room above a shop for the owner and his family to reside in (p. 6.) A possession of sixty books was a token of \u2018wealth and luxury\u2019 (p. 59.) None of this is surprising, necessarily, but such notices imprint themselves on the mind at least for awhile.

It surprised me to learn that Chaucer besmirched his verse with indecency (pp. xvi, 30), even while inditing on the subject of judgment from God (pp. 9, 151.) Considering that he was nevertheless morally awakened enough to intimate in favor of Church reform (pp. 67, 68), it is no wonder that he came to regret the polluted aspects of his work (p. 156) and that he was smitten, not long before dying, with a sense of guilt on account of his \u2018bawdy poems\u2019 (p. 162.) The shrewd reader will recognize that Peter Ackroyd tries in vain to rationalize these facts away (pp. 157, 163.) Every writer should have his deathbed in view when flinging words upon the world.

Peter Ackroyd quotes just a word or two of Chaucer\u2019s puerile filth, which is remarkable in light of his defense of it. He has put together a neat little biography, which is, if he got around to it, the first part of a \u2018series of short biographies\u2019 (back cover.) This commencement volume is a pretty good start. When presenting material that may be rumor as much as fact, Ackroyd\u2019s choice of expression is guarded: \u2018there may be some truth in this\u2019 (p. 13), \u2018one possible source of instruction\u2019 (p. 14), et cetera. He summarizes a text very well (pp. 102-103.) He knows how to introduce a Latin phrase and teach it at the same time (p. 113.) He puts forth a pleasing style: \u2018this comic poem of fickle fate\u2019 (p. 80.) He is not quite, in his own words about his Subject, \u2018a great magpie of other men\u2019s words\u2019 (p. 153.) Neither is his book, in his own words about Chaucer\u2019s verse, a \u2018considerable example and not inconsiderable success\u2019 (p. 123.) But Ackroyd\u2019s Chaucer is not bad.

There are a few weak moments. I came upon two distinctions without a difference. What difference can there be between \u2018easy\u2019 terms and \u2018familiar\u2019 terms? (p. 38.) What is the difference between \u2018vocation\u2019 and \u2018employment\u2019? (p. 44.) And there are scarce instances of phrasing lacking sense. What can \u2018the sentence of posterity for rape\u2019 mean, for example? (p. 84.)

Chaucer\u2019s poetry was often delivered orally (p. 61.) What contributes to the difficulty of understanding his words on the page is the absence of gesture and expression\u2014in short: the acting out (p. 108.) If you have been as bewildered by Chaucer\u2019s verse as some of the women who listened to him appear to have been, then this short story of his life is something that you might appreciate. The brief excerpts communicate his meaning and rhythm. The longer excerpts, though at the expense of rhythm, are modernized to at least convey his meaning. Longer extracts are isolated from the narrative to indicate that their modern translations are to be found at the back of the book. Some of these may be easily read, nevertheless, without help from the back: \u201cThe lyf so short, the craft so long to lerne,/Th\u2019assay so hard, so sharp the conqueryng\u201d (p. 88.) Through experimentation and by the influence of Petrarch, Boccaccio, and Dante (pp. 44, 45), Chaucer achieved this \u2018high style,\u2019 which later poets were to imitate for centuries.

The extract from page 88 seems to be about what is elsewhere termed \u2018the craft of fyn lovynge\u2019 (p. 32.) But it is a fit description of the arc and process of Chaucer\u2019s composition. \u201cEpilogues are cancelled\u2026prologues are rewritten\u2026lines from one tale\u2026added to another\u201d (p. 141.) Struggling writers will be relieved to read that. They will be interested to learn, moreover, that Chaucer relied on several tactics to carry out his writing ambitions. He fashioned characters from what he observed in certain people (p. 46.) Sometimes he conveyed credibility by injecting himself into his work (p. 145.) He disarmed criticism by self-deprecation (p. 144.) And he may have feigned more ignorance in his subject than was the case in order to come across more remarkably (p. 86.) Given the fact that a criminal could, in that day, escape the death penalty on the ground of being able to read (pp. 135, 136), a writer, especially one as talented as Chaucer was, must have been a man set apart from the crowd.

Chaucer had the advantage of being born of well-to-do parents, and served the royal court from his youth onward (pp. xiii, 1.) The fringe benefits that he enjoyed from the crown were many: rent-free property, grants, and annuities, for example (pp. 46, 52.) As \u2018controller of the wool custom,\u2019 he had one of the plum jobs of the day (p. 49.) That said, plum job appointments were not as mellow as they now are. Overseeing the wool trade \u2018was not an easy sinecure\u2019 (Ibid.) And that was only one role out of many that he filled. Some of the business that he did for the crown was positively perilous. What can \u2018clandestine diplomatic business\u2019 (p. 25) mean but that he was a spy for the king? Once he was even captured on the battlefield (p. 19.) During a royal disruption some of his peers were beheaded (p. 129.) The Chaucer who emerges from this biography is not the soft, merely bookish man that I supposed him to have been. He was a daring court official who wrote poetry on the side (p. 62.) Fourteenth century England was a tough, dangerous, and fairly lawless place and period (pp. 2, 10, 40, 41, 81, 83, 96, 97, 128, 154.) Chaucer was in the thick of it with everyone else.

Facts dropped in here and there by the author cause us to involuntarily compare Chaucer\u2019s England with where we now are so many years hence. The \u2018Stocks market\u2019 used to be about \u2018fish and flesh\u2019 (p. 5.) A penthouse used to be a room above a shop for the owner and his family to reside in (p. 6.) A possession of sixty books was a token of \u2018wealth and luxury\u2019 (p. 59.) None of this is surprising, necessarily, but such notices imprint themselves on the mind at least for awhile.

It surprised me to learn that Chaucer besmirched his verse with indecency (pp. xvi, 30), even while inditing on the subject of judgment from God (pp. 9, 151.) Considering that he was nevertheless morally awakened enough to intimate in favor of Church reform (pp. 67, 68), it is no wonder that he came to regret the polluted aspects of his work (p. 156) and that he was smitten, not long before dying, with a sense of guilt on account of his \u2018bawdy poems\u2019 (p. 162.) The shrewd reader will recognize that Peter Ackroyd tries in vain to rationalize these facts away (pp. 157, 163.) Every writer should have his deathbed in view when flinging words upon the world.

Peter Ackroyd quotes just a word or two of Chaucer\u2019s puerile filth, which is remarkable in light of his defense of it. He has put together a neat little biography, which is, if he got around to it, the first part of a \u2018series of short biographies\u2019 (back cover.) This commencement volume is a pretty good start. When presenting material that may be rumor as much as fact, Ackroyd\u2019s choice of expression is guarded: \u2018there may be some truth in this\u2019 (p. 13), \u2018one possible source of instruction\u2019 (p. 14), et cetera. He puts forth a pleasing style: \u2018this comic poem of fickle fate\u2019 (p. 80.) He is not quite, in his own words about his Subject, \u2018a great magpie of other men\u2019s words\u2019 (p. 153.) What can \u2018the sentence of posterity for rape\u2019 mean, for example? (p. 84.)

Chaucer\u2019s poetry was often delivered orally (p. 61.) What contributes to the difficulty of understanding his words on the page is the absence of gesture and expression\u2014in short: the acting out (p. 108.) If you have been as bewildered by Chaucer\u2019s verse as some of the women who listened to him appear to have been, then this short story of his life is something that you might appreciate. QUERY:
Which English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category have a perfect average rating of 5.0? Return all matching books.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

1. review_database

   - This database consists of one table:
    - review_database:
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Book title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

2. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)

        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)

        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review_query.db refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_database.

He fashioned characters from what he observed in certain people (p. 46.) Sometimes he conveyed credibility by injecting himself into his work (p. 145.) He disarmed criticism by self-deprecation (p. 144.) And he may have feigned more ignorance in his subject than was the case in order to come across more remarkably (p. 86.) Given the fact that a criminal could, in that day, escape the death penalty on the ground of being able to read (pp. 135, 136), a writer, especially one as talented as Chaucer was, must have been a man set apart from the crowd.

Chaucer had the advantage of being born of well-to-do parents, and served the royal court from his youth onward (pp. xiii, 1.) The fringe benefits that he enjoyed from the crown were many: rent-free property, grants, and annuities, for example (pp. 46, 52.) As \u2018controller of the wool custom,\u2019 he had one of the plum jobs of the day (p. 49.) That said, plum job appointments were not as mellow as they now are. Overseeing the wool trade \u2018was not an easy sinecure\u2019 (Ibid.) And that was only one role out of many that he filled. Some of the business that he did for the crown was positively perilous. What can \u2018clandestine diplomatic business\u2019 (p. 25) mean but that he was a spy for the king? Once he was even captured on the battlefield (p. 19.) During a royal disruption some of his peers were beheaded (p. 129.) The Chaucer who emerges from this biography is not the soft, merely bookish man that I supposed him to have been. He was a daring court official who wrote poetry on the side (p. 62.) A possession of sixty books was a token of \u2018wealth and luxury\u2019 (p. 59.) None of this is surprising, necessarily, but such notices imprint themselves on the mind at least for awhile.

It surprised me to learn that Chaucer besmirched his verse with indecency (pp. xvi, 30), even while inditing on the subject of judgment from God (pp. 9, 151.) Considering that he was nevertheless morally awakened enough to intimate in favor of Church reform (pp. 67, 68), it is no wonder that he came to regret the polluted aspects of his work (p. 156) and that he was smitten, not long before dying, with a sense of guilt on account of his \u2018bawdy poems\u2019 (p. 162.) Peter Ackroyd quotes just a word or two of Chaucer\u2019s puerile filth, which is remarkable in light of his defense of it. The third option seemed best for me. Peter Ackroyd\u2019s Chaucer is brief enough to breeze through, yet inclusive enough to satisfy one\u2019s curiosity.

This is a biography. But it is a good choice where Chaucer\u2019s verse is concerned because his verse is so often woven into his story by the biographer, and in ways that a comprehension of that verse is guaranteed. Ackroyd accomplishes this in two ways. For shorter extracts, the ones that are chosen need no modernizing, like \u201c\u2019the swyftnesse and the sweighe\u2019 of worldly fortune\u2019s \u2018turnynge wheel\u2019\u201d (p. 105) and \u201c\u2019as stil as any ston\u2019\u201d (p. 107.) Longer extracts are isolated from the narrative to indicate that their modern translations are to be found at the back of the book. Some of these may be easily read, nevertheless, without help from the back: \u201cThe lyf so short, the craft so long to lerne,/Th\u2019assay so hard, so sharp the conqueryng\u201d (p. 88.) Through experimentation and by the influence of Petrarch, Boccaccio, and Dante (pp. 44, 45), Chaucer achieved this \u2018high style,\u2019 which later poets were to imitate for centuries.

The extract from page 88 seems to be about what is elsewhere termed \u2018the craft of fyn lovynge\u2019 (p. 32.) But it is a fit description of the arc and process of Chaucer\u2019s composition. \u201cEpilogues are cancelled\u2026prologues are rewritten\u2026lines from one tale\u2026added to another\u201d (p. 141.) Struggling writers will be relieved to read that. They will be interested to learn, moreover, that Chaucer relied on several tactics to carry out his writing ambitions. He fashioned characters from what he observed in certain people (p. 46.) Sometimes he conveyed credibility by injecting himself into his work (p. 145.) He disarmed criticism by self-deprecation (p. 144.) And he may have feigned more ignorance in his subject than was the case in order to come across more remarkably (p. 86.) Given the fact that a criminal could, in that day, escape the death penalty on the ground of being able to read (pp. 135, 136), a writer, especially one as talented as Chaucer was, must have been a man set apart from the crowd.

Chaucer had the advantage of being born of well-to-do parents, and served the royal court from his youth onward (pp. xiii, 1.) The fringe benefits that he enjoyed from the crown were many: rent-free property, grants, and annuities, for example (pp. 46, 52.) As \u2018controller of the wool custom,\u2019 he had one of the plum jobs of the day (p. 49.) That said, plum job appointments were not as mellow as they now are. Overseeing the wool trade \u2018was not an easy sinecure\u2019 (Ibid.) And that was only one role out of many that he filled. Some of the business that he did for the crown was positively perilous. What can \u2018clandestine diplomatic business\u2019 (p. 25) mean but that he was a spy for the king? Once he was even captured on the battlefield (p. 19.) During a royal disruption some of his peers were beheaded (p. 129.) The Chaucer who emerges from this biography is not the soft, merely bookish man that I supposed him to have been. He was a daring court official who wrote poetry on the side (p. 62.) Fourteenth century England was a tough, dangerous, and fairly lawless place and period (pp. 2, 10, 40, 41, 81, 83, 96, 97, 128, 154.) Chaucer was in the thick of it with everyone else.

Facts dropped in here and there by the author cause us to involuntarily compare Chaucer\u2019s England with where we now are so many years hence. The \u2018Stocks market\u2019 used to be about \u2018fish and flesh\u2019 (p. 5.) A penthouse used to be a room above a shop for the owner and his family to reside in (p. 6.) A possession of sixty books was a token of \u2018wealth and luxury\u2019 (p. 59.) None of this is surprising, necessarily, but such notices imprint themselves on the mind at least for awhile.

It surprised me to learn that Chaucer besmirched his verse with indecency (pp. xvi, 30), even while inditing on the subject of judgment from God (pp. 9, 151.) Considering that he was nevertheless morally awakened enough to intimate in favor of Church reform (pp. 67, 68), it is no wonder that he came to regret the polluted aspects of his work (p. 156) and that he was smitten, not long before dying, with a sense of guilt on account of his \u2018bawdy poems\u2019 (p. 162.) The shrewd reader will recognize that Peter Ackroyd tries in vain to rationalize these facts away (pp. 157, 163.) Every writer should have his deathbed in view when flinging words upon the world.

Peter Ackroyd quotes just a word or two of Chaucer\u2019s puerile filth, which is remarkable in light of his defense of it. He has put together a neat little biography, which is, if he got around to it, the first part of a \u2018series of short biographies\u2019 (back cover.) This commencement volume is a pretty good start. When presenting material that may be rumor as much as fact, Ackroyd\u2019s choice of expression is guarded: \u2018there may be some truth in this\u2019 (p. 13), \u2018one possible source of instruction\u2019 (p. 14), et cetera. He summarizes a text very well (pp. 102-103.) He knows how to introduce a Latin phrase and teach it at the same time (p. 113.) He puts forth a pleasing style: \u2018this comic poem of fickle fate\u2019 (p. 80.) He is not quite, in his own words about his Subject, \u2018a great magpie of other men\u2019s words\u2019 (p. 153.) Neither is his book, in his own words about Chaucer\u2019s verse, a \u2018considerable example and not inconsiderable success\u2019 (p. 123.) But Ackroyd\u2019s Chaucer is not bad.

There are a few weak moments. I came upon two distinctions without a difference. What difference can there be between \u2018easy\u2019 terms and \u2018familiar\u2019 terms? (p. 38.) What is the difference between \u2018vocation\u2019 and \u2018employment\u2019? (p. 44.) And there are scarce instances of phrasing lacking sense. What can \u2018the sentence of posterity for rape\u2019 mean, for example? (p. 84.)

Chaucer\u2019s poetry was often delivered orally (p. 61.) What contributes to the difficulty of understanding his words on the page is the absence of gesture and expression\u2014in short: the acting out (p. 108.) If you have been as bewildered by Chaucer\u2019s verse as some of the women who listened to him appear to have been, then this short story of his life is something that you might appreciate. The brief excerpts communicate his meaning and rhythm. The longer excerpts, though at the expense of rhythm, are modernized to at least convey his meaning.
